segments.paradiso-28-g75: E poi che = a temporal conjunction: 'and after/when'. 'le parole sue' places the possessive after the noun (poetic order) meaning 'her words'. 'restaro' is the passato remoto (3rd person plural) of restare, here used to mean 'they came to rest' or 'fell silent' and sets the time for the following action.
segments.paradiso-28-g76: 'non altrimenti' = 'no differently / not otherwise'. 'ferro' is the grammatical subject ('iron' or a piece of iron). 'disfavilla' is the 3rd person (here past/historical) form of disfavillare 'to send forth sparks'. The following 'che' functions as a comparative 'than/when', and 'bolle' (present or vivid poetic tense) literally 'boils/seethes', used figuratively for glowing, so the clause compares the sparks to those when iron 'boils'.
segments.paradiso-28-g77: 'come' = 'as'. 'i cerchi' = 'the circles' (here the concentric angelic rings); the noun precedes the verb in normal order. 'sfavillaro' is passato remoto, 3rd person plural of sfavillare 'to sparkle / to flash', completing the simile: '[just] as the circles flashed.'
segments.paradiso-28-g78: 'L’incendio suo' places the possessive after the noun (poetic order) = 'its/her blaze'. 'seguiva' is the imperfect (3rd sing.) of seguire, here meaning 'followed' in a continuous/past-descriptive sense. 'ogne scintilla' = 'every spark' (ogne is an archaic form of ogni).
segments.paradiso-28-g79: 'ed' = 'and' (e + vowel). 'eran' is the imperfect (3rd pl.) of essere 'they were', used to introduce the quantity; 'tante' = 'so many' (agrees with the implicit sparks) and intensifies the following comparative clause.
segments.paradiso-28-g80: This is a result/comparison clause introduced by 'che' = 'that/so that'. '’l numero loro' = 'their number' (numero + possessive 'loro'). 'più che' = 'more than'. '’l doppiar de li scacchi' = 'the doubling of the chessboard/squares' (doppiar used as 'doubling'). 's’inmilla' is reflexive-formative + inmilla (Dante's verb meaning 'becomes a thousandfold' or 'is multiplied a thousand times'); together it says their number exceeds even that enormous doubling by a thousandfold.
segments.paradiso-28-g81: 'Io sentiva' uses sentire in the imperfect (1st sing.) meaning 'I heard'. After a verb of perception, Dante uses the infinitive 'osannar' = 'to cry hosanna' (object of 'sentiva') — so 'I heard (them) cry hosanna'. 'di coro in coro' is an adverbial phrase 'from choir to choir'. 'al punto fisso' = 'at the fixed point' and begins the phrase that the relative 'che' will pick up.
segments.paradiso-28-g82: 'e terrà sempre' = 'and it will hold always'. 'terrà' is the future (3rd sing.) of tenere, here asserting permanence ('will keep/maintain'). 'sempre' = 'always', emphasizing continuity.
segments.paradiso-28-g83: 'ne’' is the contracted pronoun 'in/of them' (locative/partitive). 'quai' is an archaic plural relative 'which/where' referring back to those places; 'sempre' again 'always'; 'fuoro' is passato remoto (3rd pl.) of essere 'they were'. The clause says essentially 'in which they always were' with the expected archaic word order.
segments.paradiso-28-g84: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Quella is a demonstrative pronoun 'that one' (feminine) serving as the subject; che is a relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing the subordinate clause. Vedëa is the imperfect indicative (3rd singular) of vedere in Dante's orthography (vedea/vedëa) meaning 'saw' or 'perceived'. I pensier dubi is a noun phrase: the article i + pensier (thoughts) with the adjective dubi (doubtful) placed after the noun in a poetic order. Ne la mia mente uses the older preposition ne = 'in/within' followed by the article + possessive to mean 'in my mind.'
segments.paradiso-28-g85: Disse is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd person singular of dire, 'said'; here it introduces direct speech (hence the following colon). It narrates a completed utterance by the previously named subject.
segments.paradiso-28-g86: I is the definite article 'the' (plural). Cerchi is the plural noun 'circles' or celestial spheres (poetic/plain word order puts noun before the adjective). Primi is the postposed adjective 'first.' T' is the clitic object pronoun ti contracted to t' (='you') placed before the auxiliary. Hanno mostrato is the compound perfect (avere + past participle mostrato) meaning 'have shown'—hanno is 3rd pl. of avere and mostrato is the past participle 'shown.' Serafi e Cherubi are the direct objects (plural names of angelic orders).
segments.paradiso-28-g56: Li is the archaic plural article 'the'. 'cerchi corporai' = 'corporeal circles' (corporai is a postpositive/poetic adjective). 'sono' is 3rd-person plural present of essere. 'ampi e arti' are contrasting adjectives (ampi = wide; arti is an archaic/poetic word here opposite to ampi, 'narrow' or 'close'). 'secondo' + noun phrase means 'according to'; 'il più e 'l men de la virtute' = 'the greater and the lesser of the virtue' (virtute = power/virtue), and the relative clause 'che si distende per tutte lor parti' ('which extends itself through all their parts') uses the reflexive si with distende (to spread/extend) and per tutte lor parti = 'through all their parts.'
segments.paradiso-28-g57: Subject 'Maggior bontà' ('greater goodness' or higher goodness) is followed by 'vuol far' — vuol is volere (3rd-singular present) meaning 'wants' or 'desires', plus the infinitive far (to make/cause) — so 'desires to cause/bring about'. 'maggior salute' is the direct object: 'greater salvation.'
segments.paradiso-28-g58: Elliptical line: 'maggior salute maggior corpo cape' strings two noun phrases and a verb. Read as 'greater salvation — a greater body cap[e]s' (cape is an archaic/poetic form of 'capere', here 3rd-singular meaning 'contains' or 'is fit for/receives'). Grammatically the verb agrees with 'maggior corpo' as subject: 'a greater body holds/receives greater salvation.'
segments.paradiso-28-g59: s’elli is the archaic contraction of 'se' + 'elli' = 'if they' (conditional 'if'); 'ha' is 3rd-singular present of avere, here 'has' (or 'possesses'); 'le parti' is the direct object 'the parts'; 'igualmente compiute' is a predicative adjective phrase meaning 'equally completed/perfected.' Together: 'if it (they) has its parts equally perfected.'
segments.paradiso-28-g60: Dunque is a connective 'therefore/so'. 'costui' = 'this one' is the subject; 'che' introduces a relative clause 'who'. 'tutto quanto' = 'all that/whatever' (tutto + quanto together intensify 'everything'); 'rape' is 3rd-singular present of rapere (to seize/take up), so 'who seizes/takes up'; 'l’altro universo' is the direct object 'the other universe'; 'seco' is an adverb 'with him/with it' indicating accompaniment.
segments.paradiso-28-g61: corrisponde is 3rd-singular present 'corresponds' and takes the prepositional object 'al cerchio' ('to the circle'). The two following relative clauses 'che più ama e che più sape' modify 'cerchio': 'that loves more and that knows more.' Note sape is an archaic/poetic 3rd-singular form of sapere ('knows').
segments.paradiso-28-g62: The short connective 'per che' here functions as 'therefore/so that', introducing the consequence or explanation that follows.
segments.paradiso-28-g63: Conditional 'se' = 'if' (introducing the condition). 'tu' is the explicit subject 'you'. 'a la virtù circonde la tua misura' literally 'you circumcise/encircle your measure to/according to virtue' — circonde is present indicative of circondare/circondere used transitively, meaning 'set/limit (your measure) by/according to virtue.'
segments.paradiso-28-g64: Negation 'non' + prepositional phrase: 'not by the appearance (parvenza) of the substances (sustanze) that appear to you round.' 'che t'appaion' is a relative clause with clitic t' = 'to you' and appaion (they appear) in 3rd-plural present; 'tonde' = 'round'.
segments.paradiso-28-g65: Main clause 'tu vederai' (future 'you will see'). 'mirabil consequenza' = 'a marvelous consequence/order'. The idiom 'di maggio a più e di minore a meno' uses 'di ... a ...' to express correspondence or progression: 'from the greater to the greater (maggio → più) and from the smaller to the smaller (minore → meno)' — i.e. a matching of degrees. 'maggio' here is an older form meaning 'greater.'
segments.paradiso-28-g66: A simple locative phrase: 'in ciascun cielo' = 'in each heaven' (in every celestial sphere). 'ciascun' is the distributive adjective 'each.'
segments.paradiso-28-g67: 'a süa intelligenza' = 'according to its own intelligence/understanding.' The preposition a here means 'with respect to' or 'according to', süa is the stressed/poetic form of sua 'its', and intelligenza = 'understanding/intellect.'
segments.paradiso-28-g68: Come begins a simile and functions as 'as/like'; rimane is present indicative (3rd sg.) 'remains' with the adjectives splendido e sereno describing the subject l’emisperio de l’aere. The article l’ is elided before emisperio and de l’aere uses the poetic genitive 'of the air/sky' to identify the celestial hemisphere.
segments.paradiso-28-g69: Quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; soffia is present indicative (3rd sg.) 'blows' with Borea as subject (the North Wind). Da quella guancia is a prepositional phrase 'from that cheek/side', and ond’ is the contracted poetic form of 'onde' = 'whence/from which'; è più leno uses the copula è plus comparative 'più' and the adjective leno (poetic/archaic) meaning 'milder' or 'more gentle' describing that side.
segments.paradiso-28-g70: Per che here introduces the consequence 'so/thereby/so that'; si is the reflexive/passive pronoun making purga and risolve apply impersonally or to the subject of the previous clause. Purga and risolve are present indicative (3rd sg.) meaning 'cleanses' and 'dissolves/dispels' respectively; la roffia is the (archaic) noun object 'the roffia' and che prima turbava is a relative clause 'which before disturbed/clouded it', with turbava in the imperfect indicating the prior state.
segments.paradiso-28-g71: Sì che introduces a result clause 'so that/so', with ’l ciel (the sky/heaven) as subject and ne referring back to what was just cleansed; ride is present indicative 'smiles/laughs' in a favorable sense. Con le bellezze d’ogne sua paroffia is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with the beauties of each of its parishes', where ogne (poetic form of ogni) and sua attribute paroffia.
segments.paradiso-28-g72: Così fec’ïo is a compact past assertion: così 'thus/so' and fec’ïo is the contracted poetic form of 'fec(i) io' = 'I did' (passato remoto / emphatic past), with the enclitic ïo spelling the subject 'I' after the elided verb.
segments.paradiso-28-g73: Poi che is a temporal conjunction 'after/when'; mi is an indirect object 'to me'; provide is a poetic/perfective form meaning 'provided/supplied' (here a past action by la donna mia). Del suo risponder chiaro is a prepositional noun phrase 'of her clear reply', where del = di + il governs risponder and chiaro qualifies it.
segments.paradiso-28-g74: E introduces the concluding simile 'and'; come stella in cielo sets the comparison 'as a star in the sky'; il ver is the substantive 'the truth' (il + ver), and si vide uses si as a reflexive/passive marker with vide (passato remoto 'was seen/appeared'), so the clause means 'and the truth was seen/appeared like a star in the sky.'
segments.paradiso-28-g41: 'Onde' is a connective adverb meaning 'therefore' or 'hence' (an inferential link to the previous lines); it stands alone at the start of the sentence to introduce the consequence.
segments.paradiso-28-g42: This whole clause is conditional: 'se' = 'if'; '’l' is the contracted article 'il' before a vowel; 'mio disir' = 'my desire' (archaic spelling 'disir'); 'dee aver fine' uses the archaic 'dee' (= moderno 'deve') + infinitive 'aver' = 'must have' or 'is to have' an 'end/fulfillment' ('fine'); 'in questo miro e angelico templo' is a prepositional phrase modifying where the end is to occur; 'che' introduces the relative clause 'which has only love and light as its boundary' ('ha' = 'has', 'per confine' = 'for/ as a limit').
segments.paradiso-28-g43: 'Udir' is the infinitive 'to hear'; 'convienmi' is the impersonal/archaic 'conviene' with the clitic 'mi' = 'it is necessary (to me)' or 'I must'; 'ancor' = 'still/yet'; 'come' = 'how'; 'l’essemplo' and 'l’essemplare' are two nouns ('the example' and 'the exemplar/model'); 'non vanno d’un modo' = literally 'do not go of one manner' → 'do not proceed in the same way' (present indicative 'vanno' = 'they go'). Note the old contraction 'd’' = 'di'.
segments.paradiso-28-g44: 'Ché' = 'for/because'; 'io per me' is emphatic 'I myself'; 'indarno' is an adverb meaning 'in vain'; 'a ciò' = 'about/with respect to that'; 'contemplo' is present tense 'I contemplate/consider'—here it expresses that the speaker has tried or is trying to consider the matter, but 'in vain.'
segments.paradiso-28-g45: 'Se' introduces a conditional 'if' (archaic usage); 'li' is the old plural article 'the'; 'tuoi diti' = 'your fingers'; 'non sono' = 'are not' (present indicative); 'a tal nodo' = 'for/at such a knot' (tal = such); 'sufficïenti' = 'sufficient' (diaeresis marks syllabic division in Dante's orthography).
segments.paradiso-28-g46: Impersonal reassurance: 'non è maraviglia' = 'it is no wonder' or 'no marvel' (present 'è' + noun 'maraviglia').
segments.paradiso-28-g47: 'Tanto' here is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'to that extent'—an intensifier that links the previous clause to the following explanation.
segments.paradiso-28-g48: 'Per' = 'in order to' (introducing purpose); 'non tentare' = 'not to try/test' (here 'tentare' = 'attempt/test' rather than 'tempt'); the phrase says the action was done to avoid attempting it.
segments.paradiso-28-g49: 'È fatto sodo' literally 'it is made firm/solid' (è + past participle 'fatto' + adjective 'sodo'); idiomatically: 'it has been made tight/secure' or 'it's been made firm'—an explanation why no attempt is needed.
segments.paradiso-28-g50: Punctuation mark: the single period ends the quoted speech and functions as the sentence terminator.
segments.paradiso-28-g51: 'Così' = 'thus/so' summarizing or concluding what preceded; 'la donna mia' = 'my lady' (Beatrice—the possessive follows the noun in Dante's order), used as a nominative phrase and followed by a semicolon.
segments.paradiso-28-g52: 'Poi disse' = 'then (she) said' (poi = then/afterwards; disse = passato remoto 'said'), introducing direct speech.
segments.paradiso-28-g53: 'Piglia' is the imperative (tu) 'take' (an instructive command); 'quel ch’io ti dicerò' = 'that which I will tell you' (relative 'ch’' = 'che', 'dicerò' is future 'I will say/tell'—here the lemma list for 'dicerò' does not show the ordinary 'tell', so see senses map).
segments.paradiso-28-g54: 'Se' = 'if' (introducing a contingent condition); 'vuo’' is the archaic clitic of 'vuoi' = 'you want' (2nd person sing.); 'saziarti' = 'to sate/satisfy you' (infinitive with clitic 'ti'), here 'if you want to satisfy yourself.'
segments.paradiso-28-g55: 'E' = 'and'; 'intorno da esso' = 'around it' (intorno = around/about; 'da' here part of the spatial expression); 't’assottiglia' is the imperative form with clitic 'ti' ('t’') + 'assottiglia' = 'make yourself thin/narrow' or 'draw yourself in' (tu-command), instructing the addressee to contract or narrow around the thing just described.
segments.paradiso-28-g24: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and opens the statement; l’ ottavo e ’l nono is an abbreviated coordinated noun phrase ('the eighth and the ninth') with the elided vowel in the article marked by the apostrophe. The semicolon stops the clause and prepares the explanatory clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-28-g25: e chiascheduno più tardo si movea is a clause with the imperfect verb movea (movéa) describing repeated or characteristic action in the past: 'and each one moved more slowly.' chiascheduno = 'each one' and si is the reflexive/impersonal marker in si movea (so: 'moved itself' → 'moved').
segments.paradiso-28-g26: secondo ch’era in numero distante più da l’uno is a subordinate clause introduced by secondo (here 'according to' / 'in proportion as') and ch’ (che) relative/introducing conjunction; era is the imperfect of essere ('was'); the phrase in numero distante più da l’uno modifies 'era' and means 'in number was more distant from the one' (i.e. the farther away by rank/number).
segments.paradiso-28-g27: e quello avea la fiamma più sincera introduces a contrastive clause: 'and that one had the more sincere/purer flame.' Avea is the imperfect of avere ('had'); la fiamma is the direct object; più sincera is a comparative adjective. The relative cui introduces a dative relative ('to which') governing men distava la favilla pura: 'to which the pure spark was less distant.'
segments.paradiso-28-g28: credo is the present indicative 'I believe' and is a parenthetical marker expressing Dante's modest opinion; it attaches to the following explanatory clause.
segments.paradiso-28-g29: però che più di lei s’invera is a causal/explanatory clause introduced by però che ('therefore/because' in this context). The construction s’invera is pronominal (si + invera) and here means 'is made true/realized' or 'is more confirmed'; lei refers back to the favilla (the spark).
segments.paradiso-28-g30: La donna mia is a simple noun phrase with the definite article + noun + possessive adjective 'my lady' (Beatrice). The order article–noun–possessive is normal in Italian poetic style and emphasizes the beloved.
segments.paradiso-28-g31: che mi vedëa in cura forte sospeso is a relative clause modifying 'la donna mia.' Vedëa is the imperfect of vedëare (here 'saw' or 'perceived') with mi as direct object ('saw me') and in cura 'in care/anxiety.' Forte sospeso is an adverbial (forte = 'very/strongly', sospeso = 'suspended/hesitating') describing Dante's state.
segments.paradiso-28-g32: disse is the perfect/simple past of dire ('said') and introduces direct speech; in narrative it marks reported speech.
segments.paradiso-28-g33: Da quel punto dipende il cielo e tutta la natura is direct speech: Da quel punto ('from that point') governs dipende (present indicative 'depend(s)'); the subject of dipende is 'il cielo e tutta la natura' (the heavens and all nature). The order is standard: circumstantial phrase + verb + subject.
segments.paradiso-28-g34: Mira is the imperative 'look' (addressing Dante) used to draw attention; quel cerchio is 'that circle'; che introduces a relative clause; più li è congiunto uses più 'more' + li (archaic dative/indirect-pronoun form referring to 'to it') and congiunto (past participle used adjectivally) meaning 'joined/connected.' Word order places the relative clause after the noun as usual.
segments.paradiso-28-g35: e sappi che ’l suo muovere è sì tosto per l’affocato amore ond’ elli è punto is a compound imperative + content clause: sappi (imperative 'know') + che introduces the statement. ’L suo muovere is a nominalized infinitive with possessive ('its movement'); è sì tosto means 'is so quick/immediate' (sì = 'so'); per + l’affocato amore gives the cause/means 'because of the burning/overheated love'; ond’ elli è punto is a relative phrase 'by which it is pricked/struck/animated' (ond' = 'from which/whereby'). Note sappi is an imperative form and muovere is a noun here (the motion).
segments.paradiso-28-g36: E io a lei is a short clause 'And I to her' (introducing Dante's reply). The preposition a + lei indicates the addressee ('to her'). The simple pronoun sequence is standard.
segments.paradiso-28-g37: «Se ’l mondo fosse posto con l’ordine ch’io veggio in quelle rote begins a conditional (Se + subjunctive): fosse is the imperfect subjunctive of essere ('were/if ... were'); posto is the past participle used in a passive/abstract sense ('placed/arranged'); ch’io veggio is a relative clause 'that I see' (veggio is an older form for 'vedo'); in quelle rote 'in those wheels' locates the order Dante perceives. The whole is 'If the world had been arranged in the order I see in those wheels...' (conditional protasis).
segments.paradiso-28-g38: sazio m’avrebbe ciò che m’è proposto is the apodosis of the conditional: avrei (here avrebb'e = conditional of avere) with sazio 'satisfied' yields 'I would be satisfied.' m’ is the indirect/affected pronoun 'me,' and ciò che m’è proposto is a nominal clause 'that which has been proposed to me.' The conditional and past participle form the polite speculative statement.
segments.paradiso-28-g39: ma nel mondo sensibile si puote veder le volte tanto più divine is a contrasting clause: ma (but); nel mondo sensibile ('in the sensible/perceptible world'); si puote veder is an impersonal construction with si + present 'puote' (old form of può) + infinitive veder = 'one can see'; le volte here means the heavenly 'turnings' or 'vaults/spheres'; tanto più divine = 'so much the more divine.'
segments.paradiso-28-g40: quant’ elle son dal centro più remote closes the comparison: quant’ (short for quanto) introduces 'as much as' or 'in proportion as'; elle son (are) dal centro più remote = 'they are the more remote from the center.' The comparative structure links distance from the center with divinity.
segments.paradiso-28-g104: In essa = 'in that (hierarchy)'; the preposition in precedes the demonstrative essa. Gerarcia (archaic spelling of gerarchia) is the subject placed after the locative phrase; son is the archaic present plural of essere. L’articulated phrase l’altre dee is the plural definite article + adjective + noun ('the other goddesses') and completes the sentence as predicate nominative.
segments.paradiso-28-g105: Prima is an adverb/ordinal placed before the noun to enumerate; Dominazioni (capitalized) is a proper-name plural naming the angelic order called the Dominations. The comma marks an enumeration rather than a full clause.
segments.paradiso-28-g106: E poi links the enumeration ('and then'); poi is a temporal/sequential adverb. Virtudi (poetic/archaic spelling of Virtù/Virtutes) is another named angelic order, here in apposition to the preceding item.
segments.paradiso-28-g107: L’ordine terzo = 'the third order' (article + noun + ordinal). Di Podestadi is a genitive/partitive phrase identifying of whom the order consists; èe is an archaic spelling of è (third-person singular present of essere) meaning 'is'. The structure states that the third rank belongs to the Podestadi.
segments.paradiso-28-g108: Poscia is an adverb 'afterwards/then'. Ne’ is the contracted form of in + the article and here introduces the locative phrase 'in the two penultimate tripudi' (tripudi = joyful dances/exultations). Principati e Arcangeli is the compound subject; si girano is a reflexive present plural verb meaning 'they turn/go round (themselves / revolve)', with si marking the reflexive/middle voice.
segments.paradiso-28-g109: L’ultimo è tutto = 'the last is entirely' (subject + verb + adverb). D’Angelici ludi is a prepositional phrase 'of angelic plays/games' with di contracted before a vowel; Angelici is an adjective modifying ludi, so the clause means the last [circle] consists wholly of angelic amusements/celebrations.
segments.paradiso-28-g110: Questi ordini di sù = 'these orders above' (demonstrative + noun + locative). Tutti is an emphatic quantifier 'all'. S’ammirano is a reflexive/pronominal present plural verb; here it functions as 'they gaze/adore' (they direct admiration upward). Note the reflexive pronoun s’ enclitic to the verb.
segments.paradiso-28-g111: E di giù = 'and from below' (di indicating origin/place with giù 'down/below'). Vincon is an archaic third-person plural present of vincere meaning 'they prevail' (not necessarily physical defeat but succeeding/prevalent). Sì is an adverb 'so' introducing the result clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-28-g112: Che introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that'. Verso Dio = 'toward God'. Tutti tirati sono uses the past participle tirati with essere to form a passive/state meaning 'are drawn/tugged', and the parallel e tutti tirano repeats the verb in active voice 'and all pull/draw' — a rhetorical antithesis (they are drawn and they draw).
segments.paradiso-28-g113: E Dïonisio = simple coordinate subject. Con tanto disio + a + infinitive is a common construction: 'with such desire to contemplate' where con tanto disio is an adverbial phrase of manner and a contemplar is the infinitive introduced by the preposition a. Si mise is reflexive passato remoto (mise) of mettere with si meaning 'he set himself' or 'he began', so the clause means he applied himself to contemplation.
segments.paradiso-28-g114: Che here introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'so that/and' meaning 'and he named them and distinguished them'. Li is the plural direct-object pronoun 'them' (here written like an article in the lemma list). Nomò and distinse are passato remoto forms 'he named' and 'he distinguished/identified'. Com’ io = com(e) io 'as I (do)', with the elision com' for poetic meter.
segments.paradiso-28-g115: Ma is an adversative conjunction ('but') introducing a contrast. Gregorio is the subject (a proper name, here the Pope Gregory). da lui is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from him' (showing separation). poi si divise: poi = 'then'; si is the reflexive clitic attached to the verb; divise is the passato remoto (poetic/historical past) 3rd-person singular of a reflexive verb 'dividersi' meaning 'withdrew/separated' (so the verb literally 'he separated himself').
segments.paradiso-28-g116: Onde here functions as a discourse connector ('whereupon' / 'therefore' / 'whence'), linking what immediately follows to the preceding action; it is an adverb/conjunction rather than the literal noun 'wave'.
segments.paradiso-28-g117: sì tosto come is an idiom meaning 'as soon as' (sì ... come = 'so ... as/that'); li occhi uses the archaic masculine plural article li before occhi ('the eyes'); aperse is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of aprire ('he opened'); the subject is implied (Gregory). in questo ciel is a locative phrase 'in this heaven/sky' placed after the verb for poetic order.
segments.paradiso-28-g118: di sé medesmo is an emphatic reflexive phrase: di + sé = 'about/at himself', medesmo ('same/selfsame') intensifies it ('himself proper'). rise is the passato remoto (3rd-person singular) of ridere in this context meaning 'smiled' or 'laughed' (he laughed/smiled at himself).
segments.paradiso-28-g119: E is the simple conjunction 'and'. se here is the conditional conjunction 'if' introducing a subordinate conditional clause (note: the provided sense list did not include this meaning). tanto secreto ver is a noun phrase 'so great a secret truth' where tanto modifies the noun; proferse is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of proferire ('he/she/it uttered'), and mortale is used as a noun meaning 'a mortal' (the human speaker) placed after the verb in poetic word order; in terra specifies location 'on earth'.
segments.paradiso-28-g120: non is the negation. voglio is 1st-person present of volere ('I want' / 'I wish'). ch' is the clitic form of che introducing a subordinate clause and here triggers the subjunctive. ammiri is present subjunctive (of ammirare) meaning 'that [you/one] be amazed' or 'that [you] marvel' — Dante uses the subjunctive after volere che.
segments.paradiso-28-g121: ché is a causal conjunction 'for/because'. chi 'l vide is a relative/indefinite clause 'who saw it' where l is the enclitic object clitic attached to vide (it = the truth). qua sù is an adverbial compound 'up here / here above'. gliel discoperse: gliel is the doubled clitic (gli + lo) meaning 'to him it'; discoperse is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of discoprire/discoprire used transitively 'he revealed/disclosed (it) to him'. con altro assai del ver di questi giri completes the sentence: con + altro assai = 'with much else', del ver = 'of the truth', di questi giri = 'about these circuits/spheres' (giri = poetic term for celestial circuits).
segments.paradiso-28-g87: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and modifies the whole clause; veloci is a plural adjective agreeing with the (implied) subject and placed after the adverb. Seguono is present indicative, 3rd person plural of seguire ('they follow'). 'i suoi vimi' is a plural noun phrase ('the/its vimi') with article + possessive; vimi is an older or poetic noun here (see note).
segments.paradiso-28-g88: Per + infinitive here introduces purpose ('in order to'); somigliarsi is the pronominal infinitive 'to resemble' (often used reflexively or with an indirect object). 'al punto' is a fixed phrase 'to the point' / 'exactly'; quanto ponno is a comparative clause meaning 'as much as they can' where ponno is present indicative, 3rd person plural (archaic spelling of possono).
segments.paradiso-28-g89: E introduces an additional statement. Posson is a contracted/poetic form of possono (3rd person plural present of potere) meaning 'they can'. 'quanto a veder' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'as far as sight is concerned' (quanto a + infinitive/nominalized verb), with veder as the infinitive 'to see'. Son is the 3rd person plural present of essere ('are'), and soblimi is an archaic spelling of 'sublimi' (adjective 'sublime').
segments.paradiso-28-g90: Quelli altri amori is a nominative plural noun phrase ('those other loves'). The relative clause che ’ntorno li vonno contains ’ntorno (poetic form of intorno, 'around') and the clitic li (object pronoun 'them') before the verb. Vonno is an older/poetic present, here meaning 'go around' / 'move about' (3rd person plural).
segments.paradiso-28-g91: Si chiaman is a reflexive/mediopassive use of chiamare in 3rd person plural: literally 'they call themselves' but idiomatically 'they are called'. Troni is the plural noun 'thrones'. 'del divino aspetto' is a genitive phrase 'of the divine aspect' (del = di + il).
segments.paradiso-28-g92: Per che introduces a result/reason clause ('by/through which' or 'so that'); l' primo ternaro is the definite phrase 'the first triad' (ternaro = group of three). Terminonno is an archaic/poetic verb form (3rd person plural) corresponding to modern terminano, meaning 'they terminate' or 'they set the boundary/finish'—the -onno ending is an older inflectional form.
segments.paradiso-28-g93: E introduces an additional clause. 'dei saper' = di + i + saper: saper (here a noun, 'knowledge') governed by the partitive genitive 'dei' ('of the knowledges' / 'of the knowledge'). The relative clause che tutti hanno diletto means 'which all have delight in' (hanno diletto: present indicative, they have delight). 'Quanto la sua veduta si profonda' = 'to the extent his sight plunges/ deepens' (si profonda is a pronominal verb 'to deepen/ plunge itself'). 'Nel vero in che si queta ogne intelletto' = 'in the truth in which every intellect rests' (si queta = 3rd person singular pronominal 'is calmed/settled').
segments.paradiso-28-g94: Quinci ('from here') is an adverbial locution. 'Si può veder' is an impersonal construction: si + modal verb può (3rd sing. of potere) + infinitive veder = 'one can see' or 'it can be seen'. 'Come si fonda' is 'how it is founded' with si used to form a mediopassive ('is founded'). 'L'esser beato' uses the infinitive esser as a substantive ('the being blessed'), and 'ne l'atto che vede' locates that being blessed 'in the act that sees' (atto = the acting seeing).
segments.paradiso-28-g95: Non negates the prepositional phrase that follows. 'In quel ch’ama' = 'in that which loves'; ch' is the clitic form of che (relative pronoun), and ama is 3rd person singular present of amare ('he/it loves'). The clause states what blessedness is not grounded in.
segments.paradiso-28-g96: Che poscia seconda: che = 'which/that'; poscia = 'afterwards' (adverb); seconda is 3rd person singular present of 'secondare' or an adjective/verb meaning 'follows' or 'comes second' here—literally 'which comes afterwards/which follows'.
segments.paradiso-28-g97: E (and) introduces a corollary. 'Del vedere' (di + il + vedere) treats 'vedere' ('seeing') as the domain under discussion. 'È misura mercede' = 'is measure and reward' — è (3rd sing. present of essere) links subject and predicate; misura here is a noun 'measure' (word order: predicate nominal), and mercede is an archaic noun meaning 'reward' or 'wages'.
segments.paradiso-28-g98: Che introduces a relative clause explaining the previous statement: 'that gives birth to grace and good will.' Grazia is 'grace' (noun); partorisce is present indicative, 3rd singular of partorire ('brings forth'); e buona voglia = 'and good will' (buona = adjective modifying voglia).
segments.paradiso-28-g99: Così ('thus') introduces the summation. 'Di grado in grado' is an iterative prepositional phrase meaning 'from degree to degree' or 'step by step', and 'si procede' is impersonal/mediopassive (si + procede, 3rd person singular of procedere) meaning 'one proceeds' or 'it proceeds'.
segments.paradiso-28-g100: L'altro ternaro is a simple nominal phrase: L' (definite article before a vowel) + altro ('other') + ternaro ('triad'/'group of three'). Ternaro is a substantive formed from tern- (three).
segments.paradiso-28-g101: The relative clause che così germoglia contains germoglia (3rd person singular present of germogliare, 'to sprout'/'to bud') describing how the triad comes forth. 'In questa primavera sempiterna' = 'in this everlasting spring' (sempiterna = perpetual/everlasting). The following subordinate 'che notturno Arïete non dispoglia' uses che as a relative pronoun; notturno Arïete = 'the nocturnal Ram/Aries' and dispoglia (3rd person sing.) is an older form meaning 'strips/robs' or 'despoils' — here 'does not despoil' the spring.
segments.paradiso-28-g102: Perpetüalemente is an adverb 'perpetually'. '“Osanna”' is the acclamation quoted. Sberna here is the verb form of sberno; in medieval usage it can mean to sound forth or to utter (though its basic sense is 'mock'/'scorn'), and it takes a direct object or accompaniment. 'Con tre melode' = 'with three melodies' (melode = plural of melodia in older spelling).
segments.paradiso-28-g103: Che introduces a relative clause modifying 'melode': 'that sound in three orders of joy'. Suonano is 3rd person plural present of suonare ('they sound/they ring'). 'In tree ordini di letizia' = 'in three orders (ranks) of joy'. Onde here functions as a connective meaning 'from which' or 'whence' (classical sense 'therefore/whence'), and s'interna is pronominal (si + interna) meaning 'it becomes internalized' or 'is made inward' (internare/internarsi in a reflexive sense).
segments.paradiso-28-g1: A temporal clause introduced by the adverbial Poscia che (’Afterward, when’). ’ncontro a la vita presente’ uses the contracted preposition ncontro a = "opposite/against" + noun phrase; the past-tense verb aperse is used in the literary (remote) sense 'opened/revealed' and takes l’ vero (the truth) as its direct object. The relative clause quella che ’mparadisa la mia mente modifies 'quella' (= 'that [thing]') and uses the poetic/archaic mparadisa (imparadisa) meaning 'made paradisal' or 'transported into paradise.'
segments.paradiso-28-g2: Simile introduced by come ('as/just as'). In lo specchio 'in the mirror' the image-comparison begins. Fiamma di doppiero is an older/poetic phrasing: fiamma = 'flame' and doppiero = 'the one following/behind' (poetic adverb/noun), so it describes the flame of the person behind. Vede colui che... is a present-tense generalizing clause 'one sees / the man who...' with colui che as subject.
segments.paradiso-28-g3: Prima che introduces a subordinate clause of time ('before'). The verb abbia is present subjunctive of avere used in a temporal subordinate (common in older Italian: 'before he has it in sight or thought'). In vista o in pensiero are parallel complements indicating perception or thought.
segments.paradiso-28-g4: E sé rivolge: reflexive sé + present rivolge ('and he turns himself') — here used to describe the immediate action of the person in the simile. Per veder = 'in order to see'; se introduces an indirect question 'whether' (per veder se...). Il vetro li dice il vero: the mirror is the grammatical subject of dice, and li functions as an indirect object pronoun 'to him' (old/dialectal order).
segments.paradiso-28-g5: E vede introduces the result: 'and he sees that...' ch’el s’accorda con esso is a relative/content clause where s’accorda (pronominal accordare) means 'it/together harmonizes/ corresponds' in the sense 'agrees with it.' The simile come nota con suo metro uses musical/metrical vocabulary: nota (note) with suo metro (its meter) — a figurative comparison showing correspondence between image and reality.
segments.paradiso-28-g6: Così introduces the comparison result linking back to the main narrative: 'so my memory recalls that...'. Si ricorda is a reflexive present (literally 'remembers itself' → 'remembers'). Ch’io feci is a subordinate clause with remote past feci (I made/did) used in narrative recollection; riguardando is a gerund 'while looking' or 'in looking at', governing ne’ belli occhi (into the beautiful eyes). Onde here functions as a relative/adverb ('from which' / 'whence'), and fece + Amor personifies Love as the agent who 'made the cord' (fece ... la corda) — a metaphor for being seized by love.
segments.paradiso-28-g7: Narrative past: com’ io mi rivolsi 'as I turned' uses the simple past rivolsi. Furon tocchi (furon = furono) is remote past passive/impersonal 'were touched/struck' and li miei 'my [eyes/parts]' is the affected object (li = plural article/old object marker). Da ciò che pare in quel volume is an ablative/agent phrase 'by that which appears in that book.'
segments.paradiso-28-g8: Quandunque introduces a concessive/temporal nuance 'whenever' and nel suo giro 'in its turning/circuit' describes how one should observe the motion; ben s’adocchi is an older idiom meaning 'is well perceived/closely examined' (s’ + adocchi reflexive usage).
segments.paradiso-28-g9: Un punto vidi is a temporal/locative expression 'at one point I saw'. Vidi is the remote past of vedere. Che introduces the following descriptive clause; raggiava (imperfect) indicates ongoing radiation of light; acuto modifies lume ('a sharp/keen light'). Sì intensifies the adjective.
segments.paradiso-28-g10: Relative clause introduced by che: 'so that the face which it ... must close.' EllI (poetic plural) is used for 'they/them' referring to the lights; affoca is a present-tense/poetic use meaning 'strikes/blinds/scorches' and chiuder conviensi is an impersonal construction 'it is necessary to close' (convieni/convie[n]si) with lo forte acume 'because of the strong sharpness' giving the cause.
segments.paradiso-28-g11: E quale stella par quinci più poca: a simple comparative clause 'and whatever star seems smallest from here' where quinci = 'from here' and più poca = 'smaller/lesser.' Par is the present indicative 'seems/appears.'
segments.paradiso-28-g12: Parrebbe is conditional 'would seem' (parere in conditional) used to express comparison/hypothesis: 'would appear the moon.' Luna is the comparative image used here.
segments.paradiso-28-g13: Locata con esso is a past participle locata ('placed/located') agreeing with luna implied; come stella con stella si collòca is a simile using the pronominal verb collocarsi 'is placed' (si collòca) to show proximity/arrangement: 'placed with it as star with star is placed.'
segments.paradiso-28-g14: Forse introduces a conjecture 'perhaps.' Cotanto quanto pare appresso = 'as much as seems nearby' (cotanto quanto = 'as much as'), appresso alo cigner uses older/coarse prepositional forms (alo = al + orthographic variation) and cigner (poetic infinitive) meaning 'to gird/encircle.' Quando + subordinate clause gives the condition 'when the vapor that carries it is densest.'
segments.paradiso-28-g15: Distante intorno al punto introduces a locative phrase 'distant around the point.' Un cerchio d'igne si girava: impersonal pronominal si + imperfect girava 'a circle of flames was turning/revolving.' Sì ratto modifies girava: 'so/so swiftly.'
segments.paradiso-28-g16: Ch’avria vinto uses the conditional perfect (avria + past participle) to express counterfactual/result ('that would have overcome'). Quel moto che più tosto il mondo cigne: relative clause 'the motion that most quickly girds/encircles the world' (note: cigne is poetic/archaic form of cinge/cingere).
segments.paradiso-28-g17: E questo era d’un altro circumcinto: straightforward narrative clause 'and this was encircled by another.' Era is imperfect/remote past used narratively, and circumcinto is past participle used adjectivally 'girded/encircled.'
segments.paradiso-28-g18: Short coordinated clause e quel dal terzo: 'and that (one) by the third' uses dal = da + il 'from/by the' to indicate successive encirclement.
segments.paradiso-28-g19: E ’l terzo poi dal quarto: another coordinate clause 'and the third then by the fourth' with poi = 'then/afterwards.' The article l before terzo is the usual medieval/poetic orthography.
segments.paradiso-28-g20: Dal quinto il quarto: inverted word order typical of poetry for rhythmic reasons; 'the fourth by the fifth' is expressed with dal + ordinal then the noun.
segments.paradiso-28-g21: E poi dal sesto il quinto completes the stacked series: 'and then the fifth by the sixth.' Poetic coordination and repeated dal + ordinal mark successive encirclements; word order is governed by metrical needs.
segments.paradiso-28-g22: Sopra seguiva il settimo: inversion typical of poetry ('above followed the seventh') with seguiva in the imperfect/past narrative. Sì sparto già di larghezza describes degree and state 'already so spread/parted in width' — sparto is an older/poetic participle/adjective 'spread/divided' and di larghezza indicates extent.
segments.paradiso-28-g23: Relative clause introduced by che: 'that Juno’s messenger would be arto to contain it whole.' Messo di Iuno is 'messenger of Juno' (messo = envoy/messenger). Contenerlo is infinitive + pronoun 'to contain it'; sarebbe is conditional 'would be'; arto is an archaic adjective meaning 'narrow/cramped' — the clause expresses inability/strain on the messenger to contain so wide a thing.

sentences.paradiso-28-s17: And when her words had come to rest, the iron sent forth sparks no differently than when it seethes, as the circles flashed.
sentences.paradiso-28-s18: Its blaze followed every spark; and there were so many that their number multiplied a thousandfold beyond the doubling of the chessboard.
sentences.paradiso-28-s19: I heard hosannas ring from choir to choir at the fixed point that holds them to the places, and it will always hold, in which they always were.
sentences.paradiso-28-s20: And she who saw the doubtful thoughts in my mind said: "The first circles have shown you Seraphim and Cherubim."
sentences.paradiso-28-s13: The corporeal circles are wide and narrow according to the greater and the less of the virtue that extends through all their parts.
sentences.paradiso-28-s14: Greater goodness wishes to bring about greater salvation; greater salvation requires (or befits) a greater body, if it has its parts equally perfected.
sentences.paradiso-28-s15: Therefore this one — who seizes up everything that the other universe takes with it — corresponds to the circle that loves more and that knows more; so, if you set your measure by virtue and not by the appearance of the substances that seem round to you, you will see a marvelous consequence: greater to greater and lesser to lesser, in each heaven, according to its own intelligence.
sentences.paradiso-28-s16: As the hemisphere of the air remains splendid and serene when Boreas blows from that cheek where it is milder, so that what had before clouded it is cleansed and dissolved and the sky smiles on it with all the beauties of each of its parishes; so I did, after my lady supplied me with her clear reply, and the truth appeared like a star in the sky.
sentences.paradiso-28-s9: Therefore, if my desire is to find an end in this wondrous and angelic temple, which has only love and light for its boundary, I must still hear how the example and the exemplar do not go in the same way, for I myself contemplate that to no purpose.
sentences.paradiso-28-s10: “If your fingers are not sufficient for such a knot, it is no wonder: so, in order not to try it, it has been made firm!”
sentences.paradiso-28-s12: Thus my lady; then she said: “Take what I will tell you, if you want to satisfy yourself; and draw yourself in around it.”
sentences.paradiso-28-s5: Thus the eighth and the ninth; and each one moved more slowly, according as it was, in number, more distant from the first; and that one had the purer flame to which the pure spark was less distant, I believe, because it is more truly made manifest in her.
sentences.paradiso-28-s6: My lady, who saw me in anxious care, greatly suspended, said: 'From that point depend the heavens and all of nature.'
sentences.paradiso-28-s7: Look at that circle which is more closely joined to it; and know that its motion is so swift because of the burning love by which it is moved.
sentences.paradiso-28-s8: And I to her: 'If the world had been placed in the order that I see in those wheels, I would be satisfied with what has been proposed to me; but in the sensible world one can see the spheres so much more divine as they are more remote from the center.'
sentences.paradiso-28-s25: In that hierarchy are the other goddesses: first the Dominations, and then the Virtues; the third order is of the Podestates.
sentences.paradiso-28-s26: Then, in the two penultimate rejoicings, Principalities and Archangels revolve; the last is entirely of angelic plays.
sentences.paradiso-28-s27: These orders above all gaze (upwards), and from below they prevail so, that toward God all are drawn and all draw.
sentences.paradiso-28-s28: And Dionysius, with such desire to contemplate these orders, set himself to it, for he named them and distinguished them as I [do].
sentences.paradiso-28-s29: But Gregory then withdrew from him; whereupon, as soon as he opened his eyes in this heaven, he smiled to himself.
sentences.paradiso-28-s30: And if a mortal on earth uttered so great a hidden truth, I do not want you to be amazed; for whoever saw it up here revealed it to him, together with much else of the truth about these circuits.
sentences.paradiso-28-s21: Thus swiftly follow its shoots, striving to resemble it at the point as much as they can; and as to sight they are as sublime as possible.
sentences.paradiso-28-s22: Those other loves that move around them are called Thrones of the divine aspect, through which the first triad finds its end; and of the knowledge in which all have delight, insofar as his sight plunges into the truth in which every intellect is at rest.
sentences.paradiso-28-s23: From this one can see how the state of blessedness is founded in the act that sees, not in that which loves (which comes afterward); and of seeing there is measure and reward, which gives birth to grace and good will: thus, from degree to degree, one proceeds.
sentences.paradiso-28-s24: The other triad, which thus sprouts in this everlasting spring that the nocturnal Ram does not strip away, perpetually intones 'Hosanna' with three melodies, which sound in three orders of joy whence it becomes inward.
sentences.paradiso-28-s1: Afterward, when the truth revealed itself against the present life of wretched mortals — that which put my mind into paradise — just as one sees in a mirror the flame of the one behind who lights himself from it, before he has it in sight or in thought, and turns himself to see whether the glass tells him the truth, and sees that it agrees with it like a note with its measure; so my memory recalls that I, looking into the beautiful eyes from which Love made the cord to take me.
sentences.paradiso-28-s2: And when I turned and my eyes were struck by what appears in that book — whenever one looks carefully at its circuit — I saw at one point a light that radiated so keen a brightness that the face which it smote had to be shut because of the fierce sharpness; and whatever star seems smallest from here would seem like the moon set beside it, placed with it as star is placed beside star.
sentences.paradiso-28-s3: Perhaps as much as seems nearby the girdle the light that paints it when the vapor that bears it is thickest; around the point a ring of flames was turning so swiftly that it would have overcome the motion that most quickly girds the world; and this was encircled by another, and that by a third, and the third then by a fourth, the fourth by a fifth, and then the fifth by a sixth.
sentences.paradiso-28-s4: Above it followed the seventh, already so wide in breadth that even Juno’s messenger would be cramped to contain it whole.
